relational-model-basics.label: Relational Model Basics
relational-model-basics.summary: Understand tables, rows, and columns in relational databases.

relational-model-overview.title: What is the Relational Model?
relational-model-overview.bodyMarkdown: The relational model is a way to organize data into tables, also known as relations. Each table consists of rows and columns. Rows represent individual records, while columns represent attributes of the data. This structure makes it easy to store, retrieve, and manipulate data efficiently.

tables-rows-columns.title: Tables, Rows, and Columns
tables-rows-columns.bodyMarkdown: A **table** is like a spreadsheet, with each **row** representing a single record (such as a customer or product), and each **column** representing a property or attribute (such as name or price). Every value in a table is stored at the intersection of a row and a column.

single-choice-table-definition.title: What best describes a table in the relational model?
single-choice-table-definition.prompt: Choose the most accurate description of a table in a relational database.
single-choice-table-definition.hint: Think about how data is organized in spreadsheets.

help.concept: A table in the relational model organizes data into rows (records) and columns (attributes).
help.hint_1: Tables are made up of rows and columns, not just a single value.
help.hint_2: Tables are not commands or graphics; they store structured data.

options.a: A collection of related data organized into rows and columns.
options.b: A single value stored in the database.
options.d: A list of commands for the database.

multi-choice-rows-columns.title: Identifying Rows and Columns
multi-choice-rows-columns.prompt: Which statements are true about rows and columns in a relational table? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-rows-columns.hint: Consider the function of rows and columns in a table.

help.concept: Rows are records; columns are attributes. Both are essential for a table.
help.hint_1: Rows and columns have different roles in a table.
help.hint_2: A table can't exist without both rows and columns.

options.a: Each row represents a single record.
options.b: Each column represents a specific attribute.
options.c: Rows and columns can be used interchangeably.
options.d: A table must have at least one row and one column.

drag-reorder-table-structure.title: Order the Elements of a Table
drag-reorder-table-structure.prompt: Arrange the following elements from the most general to the most specific in a relational database.
drag-reorder-table-structure.hint: Start with the largest structure and move to the smallest.

help.concept: A table contains rows and columns, and each cell is at the intersection of a row and a column.
help.hint_1: Think about what contains what: tables contain rows and columns.
help.hint_2: A cell is the smallest unit, found at the intersection of a row and a column.

fill-blank-choice-table-example.title: Fill in the Blank: Table Example
fill-blank-choice-table-example.prompt: In a table of students, each ______ represents a student, and each ______ represents information like 'name' or 'age'.
fill-blank-choice-table-example.hint: Think about which part stores the record and which part stores the attribute.

help.concept: Rows are records (students), columns are attributes (name, age).
help.hint_1: A row is a single record; a column is a property.
help.hint_2: Each student is a row; 'name' or 'age' is a column.

fill-blank-choice-table-example.template: In a table of students, each [[blank1]] represents a student, and each [[blank2]] represents information like 'name' or 'age'.

code-input-select-all-products.title: Write a Query: Select All Rows
code-input-select-all-products.prompt: Write an SQL query to select all columns and rows from the 'products' table in the 'products_catalog' database.
code-input-select-all-products.hint: Use SELECT with * to get all columns.

help.concept: The SELECT statement retrieves data from a table. Using * selects all columns.
help.hint_1: The basic form is SELECT * FROM table_name;
help.hint_2: Remember to use the correct table name: 'products'.
